coredata.dc:title: Preventive conservation of paper-based relics with visible light high-transmittance ultraviolet blocking film based on carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mingliang
Zhao, Jinchan
Wang, Sinong
Dai, Zhenyu
Qin, Shuaitao
Mei, Shiliang
Zhang, Wanlu
Guo, Ruiqian
coredata.dc:description: Paper-based relics is an important carrier for recording and preserving information, however, it faces irreversible UV-induced damage, including photocleavage, oxidation, acidification and discoloration, which seriously affects its value and lifespan. Carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent UV absorption and good chemical stability, making them suitable for UV protection. Herein, we propose a high-security and efficient method utilizing CDs films (CDFs) for preventive protection of paper against UV damage. The CDFs with high tunable UV absorbance and minimal absorbance in the visible light range, effectively shield paper from UV radiation while preserving its visual appeal. Moreover, the UV transmittance of the film can be fine-tuned to the content of CDs and can be easily removed from the paper without residue. Artificial accelerated UV aging experiments demonstrate the deceleration of acidification, oxidation, and photocleavage in the protected bamboo paper and Xuan paper. This research paces a new direction for the protection of paper and paper-based relics and artworks with emerging carbon materials, offering customizable protection effects tailored to specific preservation and exhibition requirements. This research pioneers a novel approach to preventive protection of paper and paper-based relics using emerging carbon dots materials, offering tailored protection for diverse preservation needs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Paper-based relics
Anti-UV degradation
Anti-acidification
Preventive conservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of visible light high-transmittance UV-blocking CDFs
Evaluation of degradation and protection of bamboo paper under UV-accelerated aging
Evaluation of degradation and protection of Xuan paper under UV-accelerated aging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (inset of (a) presents HRTEM image). (b) XRD pattern of CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (d-f) C 1s, O 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of CDs.

(a) UV–vis and PLE spectra of CDs (0.02 mg/ml). (b) UV–vis spectra of CDs with different concentrations. (c) PL emission of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs. (e) PL intensity of CDs on repeated cycling between 25 °C and 75 °C. (f) PL intensity of CDs at different pH values.

(a) Photographs of UV-induced color change card covering CDF in sunlight. (b) UV–vis spectra of CDF containing varying concentrations of CDs. (c) Photographs of CDF in daylight. (d) b* and ΔE* values of CDF.

(a) pH values of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % during the UV aging process. (b) ΔE* values of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % during the UV aging process. (c) Optical images of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % during the UV aging process. (d-f) FTIR spectra, UV–vis spectra and XPS survey spectra of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % after UV aging for 20 d. (g-i) C 1s XPS spectra of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % after UV aging for 20 d.

(a) pH values of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % during the UV aging process. (b) ΔE* of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % during the UV aging process. (c) Optical images of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % during the UV aging process. (d-f) FTIR spectra, UV–vis spectra and XPS survey spectra of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % after UV aging for 20 d. (g-i) C 1s XPS spectra of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % after UV aging for 20 d.
Illustration for the preparation of carbon dot films and their application in paper preventive protection.

head.ce:title: Preventive conservation of paper-based relics with visible light high-transmittance ultraviolet blocking film based on carbon dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Electric Light Sources, School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paper-based relics is an important carrier for recording and preserving information, however, it faces irreversible UV-induced damage, including photocleavage, oxidation, acidification and discoloration, which seriously affects its value and lifespan. Carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent UV absorption and good chemical stability, making them suitable for UV protection. Herein, we propose a high-security and efficient method utilizing CDs films (CDFs) for preventive protection of paper against UV damage. The CDFs with high tunable UV absorbance and minimal absorbance in the visible light range, effectively shield paper from UV radiation while preserving its visual appeal. Moreover, the UV transmittance of the film can be fine-tuned to the content of CDs and can be easily removed from the paper without residue. Artificial accelerated UV aging experiments demonstrate the deceleration of acidification, oxidation, and photocleavage in the protected bamboo paper and Xuan paper. This research paces a new direction for the protection of paper and paper-based relics and artworks with emerging carbon materials, offering customizable protection effects tailored to specific preservation and exhibition requirements. This research pioneers a novel approach to preventive protection of paper and paper-based relics using emerging carbon dots materials, offering tailored protection for diverse preservation needs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chinese handmade paper, since its invention, has served as the primary medium for recording, preserving, and disseminating knowledge and ideas throughout history, carrying significant cultural value. It revolutionized the way of information transmission and storage, advancing civilization and being widely used in the writing and printing of heritage assets, including Chinese ancient books, calligraphy and painting. The main component of paper is cellulose, which is connected by β-glycosidic bonds to form the basic structure of paper. However, both internal factors, such as acidic pulping during paper formation, and external factors, including light exposure, air pollutants, and mold in preservation environments can lead to cellulose degradation, potentially causing the destruction and loss of valuable paper resources and paper-based cultural heritage [1,2]. For example, ultraviolet (UV) light can directly trigger photocleavage of cellulose and other constituent molecules (e.g., hemicellulose, lignin), posing a serious threat to paper preservation [3]. Meanwhile, photo-oxidation worsens the degradation process [4]. Chromogenic groups in paper absorb UV light, generating free radicals, which initiate chain reactions resulting in the formation of carboxyl groups and the breaking of the cellulose main chain. Consequently, paper is susceptible to acidification, oxidation, discoloration, and other forms of deterioration, greatly reducing its mechanical strength and lifetime expectancy. Given these challenges, protecting Chinese handmade paper and paper-based relics from UV damage is of paramount importance.

Researches have been conducted on solutions for UV protection. For example, lignin has gained attention for its natural UV-absorbing properties within the wavelength range of 250–400 nm, making it suitable for various applications such as sunscreen creams, transparent films, paints, varnishes, and microorganism protection [5]. Furthermore, nanomaterials like ZnO, TiO2, CeO2, SiO2, Cu NPs, CDs, and plant extracts are widely used in UV-blocking applications [6]. Recent efforts have focused on addressing UV protection for paper and paper-based relics. Graphene has emerged as a promising material for shielding paper from UV radiation, with studies demonstrating its effectiveness in reducing artwork fading [7]. Graphene oxide has also been used as a UV-protecting agent for Xuan paper through surface application via spraying [8]. Moreover, TiO2 has been investigated for its UV-absorbing ability and chemical stability, with nanocoating proposed for UV blocking of paper [3]. Due to the fragile, valuable, and culturally significant nature of paper and paper-based relics, UV protective materials need to be safe, easily prepared, and the protection method should avoid causing fiber swelling, fading and discoloration of handwriting, secondary degradation (e.g., photosensitive reaction and alkaline degradation), etc., as well as meet the principles of minimum intervention and reversibility in cultural relics preservation. These high standards of protection limit the variety of materials available, and the development of safe and efficient protection materials and methodologies is crucial.

Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials (<10 nm), offer an innovative solution due to their inherent UV absorption, excellent photostability, simple preparation, nontoxicity and rich raw materials [9]. Since their discovery in 2004 [10], CDs have demonstrated versatile utility across various domains such as photocatalysis [11–13], LED technology [14,15], anti-counterfeiting measures [16–18], and imaging [19–21]. Presently, CDs have emerged as a valuable resource in UV protection. For example, carbon dots derived from microcrystalline cellulose were used to create UV-blocking films by blending them with poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), effectively protecting wood from UV-induced damage [22]. Cellulose-encapsulated carbon dots were prepared into biodegradable, transparent and flexible films for blocking UV and high-energy blue light, providing protection for eyes and skin [23]. Our recent researches have successfully applied CDs with outstanding UV absorption and photostability for paper protection through aqueous phase protection method [24]. The protected-paper exhibits enhanced stability under UV aging and dry heat aging conditions, displaying reduced oxidative and acidic degradation rates. Furthermore, CDs-based 0D/2D hierarchical nanohybrids can be simply prepared by in-situ growth strategy to enhance and diversify paper protection capabilities [25]. These meaningful results demonstrate the new applications of CDs as an emerging material in the field of paper conservation. To meet the rigorous standards for the protection of paper and paper-based relics, in this study, we pioneer a preventive method for UV protection of paper using CDs films (CDF) (Scheme 1). These films, prepared through hydrothermal synthesis of CDs followed by the mixing and drying of CDs with PVA, exhibit high tunable UV absorbance while maintaining minimal absorbance in the visible light range. Consequently, they effectively shield paper from UV damage while preserving the visual appeal of paper and paper-based artworks. Moreover, these protective films can be easily removed from paper surfaces without leaving any residue, in contrast to currently available polymer-coated and liquid-phase impregnated materials, thus addressing the challenge of providing high-security and efficient protection for paper, precious paper-based relics and artworks.

Ethylene imine polymer (PEI, MW 1800, 99 %) and o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical reagent) were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA, ≥99.5 %) was obtained from Macklin Aladdin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol 124 (PVA, guaranteed reagent) was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The traditional handmade bamboo paper (BP, made with bamboo fibers produced in Jiangxi Province in 2018, pH ∼ 6.1, 28.6 g/m2) and Xuan paper (XP, made with blue sandalwood and rice straw produced in Anhui Province in 2019, pH ∼ 6.9, 22.9 g/m2) were selected for paper protection experiments.

CA (1 g), PEI (0.5 g) and OPD (0.5 g) were dissolved in deionized water (20 ml). The solution was then transferred to a para-phenylene hydrothermal synthesis reactor and reacted at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged (8000 rpm for 5 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane to remove undissolved residues. The supernatant was then dialyzed in deionized water for 48 h for further purification (molecular weight cutoff: 500 Da). The resulting CDs powder (0.11 g) was obtained by freeze-drying.

The CDF was prepared by a simple solvent casting method. Specifically, the as-prepared CDs powder (0 mg, 0.25 mg, 1.25 mg, 2.5 mg, 12.5 mg, and 25 mg) and PVA (0.5 g) were dissolved in deionized water (20 ml). The mixture was stirred at 90 ℃ for 30 min until transparent. Subsequently, the transparent solution was transferred to a plastic culture dish with a diameter of nine centimeters and dried at 60 ℃ to form the CDF.

BP and XP were cut into several 2 × 2 cm2 pieces. These paper pieces were then covered with CDF on the surface. Subsequently, they were subjected to artificial accelerated aging in a chamber equipped with a UV lamp operating at a wavelength of 340 nm and a power of 40 W for UV aging. The distance between the paper surface and the UV lamp plane was maintained at 21 cm. The temperature during this process was controlled at 25 ± 2 ℃, with humidity kept at a level of 65 ± 5 %. As a control group, paper pieces without CDF were also placed in the chamber for artificial accelerated aging. BP and XP underwent accelerated aging for 5, 10, and 20 d, respectively, before being used for characterization.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were characterized by a HT7700 Exalens field emission microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were recorded by a D8 ADVANCE ray diffractometer. The fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 FTIR Spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer. The binding energy calibration was based on C 1s at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs solution were tested using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (759S). And the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs and CDF were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97XP). Fluorescence decay curves of CDs were constructed with a Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra of CDF were determined by a Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The pH values of paper were obtained by a SANXIN pH5F pH meter. The L*, a* and b* were acquired on an NR10QC colorimeter, where L*, a*, and b* represents lightness, colour between magenta and green, and colour between yellow and blue, respectively. The ΔE* relative to the original paper was calculated using Formula S1 in the supporting information. The optical images were obtained by an AM4115ZT of Dino-Lite. And Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of paper were recorded on an ALPHA of BRUKER FTIR Spectrometer. The degree of polymerization (DP) is determined according to GB/T 1548-2016.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of visible light high-transmittance UV-blocking CDFs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were initially synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method at 180 ℃. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. The particle size distribution of CDs ranges from 1.0 nm to 2.4 nm, exhibiting a uniform distribution with an average size of about 1.6 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice plane of graphite carbon [26]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows a broad peak at 22.3° corresponding to the (002) crystal face of CDs (Fig. 1b) [27,28]. The fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) analysis was conducted to detect the functional groups of CDs, as shown in Fig. 1c. The broad band observed from 3500 cm−1 to 3180 cm−1 corresponds to OH and NH stretching vibrations, while peaks ranging from 2780 cm−1 to 3130 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH [29,30]. The absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 is indicative of CO/CN, whereas the strong peak at 1560 cm−1 is attributed to NH bending, and the peak at 1444 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibration [31,32]. The peak at 1350 cm−1 corresponds to aromatic CN stretching vibration, and the peak at 1294 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibration on the aromatic ring [33,34]. The surface groups of CDs were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey spectrum (Fig. S1) shows peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, indicating that CDs are mainly composed of C, O, and N elements. In the high-resolution spectra (Fig. 1d-f), the C 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to CC (284.8 eV), CN/CO (286.2 eV) and CO (287.8 eV), respectively [26]. The O 1s band contains two peaks at 530.7 eV and 531.7 eV for CO and CO, respectively, while the N 1s peak is the sum of the pyridinic N (399.2 eV) and the pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) [35]. The results of FTIR and XPS spectra indicate the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, amino, and amide groups, contribute to the diverse range of surface states and enhance the UV absorption capacity [22].

The optical properties of CDs aqueous solution were investigated using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE), and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV–vis spectrum shows two strong absorption bands around 240 nm and 360 nm, corresponding to π-π* transition originating from sp2-hybridized carbon and n-π* transition related to CO and surface functional groups of CDs, respectively [36]. The UV absorption is strong in the wavelength range below 400 nm and weak in the wavelength range above 400 nm, indicating that CDs can efficiently absorb ultraviolet rays with minimal absorption of visible light, which is important for the protection of paper-based relics and artworks without compromising their viewing pleasure. Fig. 2b illustrates the variation in UV absorption characteristics of CDs with concentration. As the concentration increases, the absorption of CDs around 360 nm increases, with the absorption band only slightly expanding towards the visible light wavelength range. The PLE spectrum exhibit a strong absorption peak at 402 nm and a band from 250 nm, suggesting that the fluorescence originates from n-π* transition. Fig. 2c displays the PL emission of CDs under different excitations, revealing their excitation-independent fluorescence property, with the maximum emission at 449 nm when excited at 405 nm. Furthermore, the average fluorescence lifetime is fitted by a double exponential function to be 2.81 ns (Fig. 2d). To assess the stability and practicality of CDs, experiments were conducted to evaluate the variation of PL intensity with temperature and pH. As shown in Fig. 2e, the PL intensity decreases as the temperature rises from 25 ℃ to 75 ℃. Nonetheless, the PL intensity recovers when the temperature returns to 25 ℃, and it remains stable after five cycles, indicating the excellent temperature stability of CDs. Fig. 2f illustrates the variation of PL intensity with pH value, showing minimal changes in PL intensity with pH and a slight increase observed with decreasing pH. This phenomenon could be attributed to proton transfer from protonated nitrogen to the conjugated carbon structure [37].

By mixing and drying the as-prepared CDs (0, 0.5 mg, 2.5 mg, 5 mg, 25 mg, 50 mg) with PVA (0.5 g), series of CDF with different CDs mass fractions (0, 0.1 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%) were successfully prepared. The thickness of CDF measured by a thickness gauge is approximately 70 μm. The UV-induced color change card visually demonstrates the UV-blocking effect of CDFs (Fig. 3 a). In the absence of UV irradiation, the color of the card is white, and under UV irradiation, the card changes to purple within a few seconds. When covered with CDF, the purple color of the card becomes significantly lighter, indicating that CDF has a significant UV-blocking effect that increases with the concentration of CDs. As Fig. 3b shows, the UV transmittance of the PVA film without CDs is notably high, however, the addition of CDs can significantly reduce UV transmittance. With increasing CDs content, the UV transmittance of CDF gradually decreases. Notably, the UV transmittance of CDF-0.5 % is only 28 %, while the visible light transmittance is 89.7 %, merely 1.2 % lower than that of the pure PVA film. This indicates that CDF-0.5 % can effectively shield most ultraviolet rays without compromising visual clarity. Moreover, the UV transmittance of CDF-10 % is as low as 0.04 %, while the visible light transmittance remains at 71.5 %. This implies that CDF-10 % can absorb nearly all ultraviolet rays, although sacrificing some reduction in visible light transmittance. Fig. 3c presents photographs of CDF under daylight conditions. When the concentration of CDs is lower than 1 %, CDF is a transparent film with minimal change in color from PVA film, CDF-1 % begins to show slight color change, and when the concentration of CDs is 5 %, there is a significant change in the color of CDF. The quantitative evaluation of this visual color change can be achieved through CIE color coordinates (L, a, b)* and colorimetric change (ΔE*), where a larger b* value represents a yellower color, and a greater ΔE* value indicates a more pronounced color change. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, when the CDs concentration is below 1 %, the increase in b* and ΔE* of CDF is minimal. However, after the CDs concentration over 1 %, there is a significant increase in both b* and ΔE* of CDF. Specifically, the ΔE* of CDF-1 % is 3.08, which is just over the range of color difference tolerance (ΔE* = 3) [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of degradation and protection of bamboo paper under UV-accelerated aging
ce:section.ce:para: In order not to affect the visual effect to protect the paper and at the same time understand the protective effect of the film, CDF-0.5 % and CDF-10 % were selected for UV protection experiments. Two representative types of traditional Chinese handmade paper, bamboo paper (BP) and Xuan paper (XP), have been selected for paper degradation and conservation research. First, BP, BP covered by CDF-0.5 % (BP-0.5 %), and BP covered by CDF-10 % (BP-10 %) were placed in a UV aging chamber for artificial accelerated aging. The pH value was used to estimate the acidic groups generated during UV aging of the paper, a lower pH indicates the generation of more acidic groups, signifying more severe degradation of the paper. After UV aging for 5, 10, and 20 d, the pH values of BP decrease from initial ∼6.1 to 5.09, 4.71, and 4.3, respectively (Fig. 4 a). Meanwhile, the pH values are 5.68, 5.36, and 4.91 for BP-0.5 %, and 5.88, 5.79, and 5.66 for BP-10 %, after UV aging for the same duration, respectively, significantly higher than those of BP. This means that CDF can effectively inhibit the acidification of bamboo paper during UV aging, with CDF-10 % exhibiting superior protective effects compared to CDF-0.5 %, which is attributed to the higher concentration of CDs in CDF-10 %, resulting in a higher UV shielding rate. Moreover, the degree of polymerization (DP) can be used to indicate the length of cellulose chains in paper. Following UV aging for 20 d, the DP values of BP, BP-0.5 %, and BP-10 % decreased to 48.3 %, 56.9 %, and 94.0 % of their initial values, respectively. The higher DP of BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % suggests that CDF slow down the photocleavage of cellulose chain in paper induced by UV irradiation. Both the results from pH and DP indicate that CDF-0.5 % can slow down the rate of UV aging of paper, while CDF-10 % can slow it down more significantly. Since UV aging causes discoloration of BP, especially yellowing, CIE color coordinates and colorimetric change (Table S1) were further used to assess photo-aging of paper. Fig. 4b shows that the ΔE* of BP increased to 3.36, 3.62 and 4.05 after 5, 10 and 20 d of UV aging, respectively, indicating paper discoloration due to UV aging. Conversely, the ΔE* of BP-0.5 % increased to 1.90, 2.37 and 2.99 for the same UV aging duration, suggesting a slower yellowing rate compared to BP. The ΔE* of BP-10 % further decreased to 0.63, 1.00, and 1.99 for the same UV aging cycle, indicating superior protection offeres by CDF-10 %. Additionally, the optical image of the paper in Fig. 4c confirms these findings, showing that after 20 d of UV aging, BP exhibited the most pronounced yellowing, while BP-10 % exhibited the lightest color, thus confirming the effectiveness of CDF in inhibiting BP yellowing.

FTIR, UV–vis and XPS spectra were employed to further examine the evolution of composition and functional groups in BP during the UV aging process. The FTIR spectra of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % are shown in Fig. 4d. The peaks at 664 cm−1 and 897 cm−1 can be assigned to out-of-plane OH deformation vibration of hydroxyl groups and COC stretching vibration of β-(1,4)-glycosidic bonds, respectively [38]. The peaks located at 1029 cm−1 and 1104 cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching of primary and secondary alcohol, respectively [39]. Moreover, the peaks centered at 1160 cm−1 and 1315 cm−1 are considered to be the COC stretching vibration and wagging vibration of CH2, respectively [38]. The absorbance peaks at 1365 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1 correspond to the CH bending and CH2 bending, respectively [39]. The strong and board bands at 2900 cm−1 and 3330 cm−1 are assigned to CH stretching and OH stretching, respectively [40]. The oxidation index (OIIR) was obtained by integrating the carbonyl-related peaks ranging from 1620 cm−1 to 1730 cm−1, normalized by an internal standard defined as the integral absorbance of the CH band at 2900 cm−1 within the range of 3000–2800 cm−1 [41]. After 20 d of UV aging, the OIIR of BP is 0.132, while the OIIR of BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % is 0.115 and 0.106, respectively. This indicates that CDF can slow down the oxidation rate of the paper, attributed to its capability to block a large number of UV rays and weaken the photo-oxidative degradation of BP. The UV–vis spectra of BP were used to further analyze the influence of CDF on UV protection (Fig. 4e and Fig. S2). The absorbance peaks at about 240, 280 and 330 nm can be assigned to the hexenuronic groups formed during alkaline pulping, lignin, and coniferylaldehyde, a major contributor to the colour of lignin [42,43]. The more prominent peaks at 240, 280 and 330 nm of BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % indicate less UV degradation of hexenuronic groups and lignin compared to BP. Moreover, the difference in the absorption band at around 500 nm may be due to the formation of carbonyl chromophores from the ring-opening reaction of anhydroglucose, which may be the reason for the yellowing of paper [44]. The intensity of the absorption band near 500 nm is higher for BP than for BP-0.5 % and BP-10 %, which further confirms the more severe oxidation and yellowing in BP without protection. Furthermore, the integration of the wavelength range from 248 nm to 500 nm was calculated as the oxidation index (OIUV–vis) of BP [44]. After UV aging for 20 d, the OIUV–vis of BP is 191, while the OIUV–vis of BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % is 188 and 184, respectively, which also illustrates the inhibitory effect of CDF on UV damage of paper. To acquire deeper insights into the UV aging process of BP, XPS detection was conducted. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4f) show the main elements of BP are carbon in the region of 285–289 eV and oxygen in the region of 532 and 533 eV, consistent with expectations. Following UV aging for 20 d, the oxygen content in BP is 69 %, which is higher than those of BP-0.5 % and BP-10 %, indicating more serious oxidation of BP. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra for C1s of BP, BP-0.5 % and BP-10 % (Fig. 4g-i) indicate four components corresponding to CC (285 eV), CO (286.7 eV), OCO/CO (288.2 eV) and OCO (289.5 eV) [45] The content of these functional groups was summarized in Table S2. After UV aging for 20 d, the total content of oxidized groups (OCO/CO and OCO) in BP is 13.28 %, whereas in BP-0.5 % and BP-10 %, it is 11.83 % and 10.31 %, respectively. The higher contents of OCO/CO and OCO in BP indicates that more carbonyl groups were formed in BP without CDF protection. The combined results of pH, colorimetric change, OIIR, OIUV–vis and XPS spectra, suggest that the rate of UV aging (including acidification, oxidation, photolysis and yellowing) of CDF-covered BP is slower than that of BP without protection, indicating the effective reduction of UV damage in BP facilitated by CDF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of degradation and protection of Xuan paper under UV-accelerated aging
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of CDF for UV protection of paper, we further selected Xuan paper, renowned for its resistance to aging, as a representative for UV aging experiments. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the pH value of XP declines from initial ∼6.9 to 6.45, 6.44 and 6.33 after UV aging for 5, 10 and 20 d, respectively. While the pH values of XP covered by CDF-0.5 % (XP-0.5 %) and XP covered by CDF-10 % (XP-10 %) remain stable at 6.41 and 6.58 after 20 d of UV aging, and the pH values of XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % are consistently higher than those of XP during the aging process. Meanwhile, the DP of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % decreases to 73.4 %, 86.1 % and 93.8 % of their respective initial values. These findings suggest that Xuan paper experiences degradation at a slower rate compared to bamboo paper, with CDF also delaying the acidification and photocleavage processes during UV aging. Additionally, the noteworthy drop in b* values (Table S3) during UV aging indicates that Xuan paper may have undergone photobleaching, which is different from the yellowing observed in bamboo paper. Fig. 5b shows the ΔE* calculated for the series of Xuan paper relative to the original XP. After UV aging for 20 d, the ΔE* of XP (1.63) is higher than those of XP-0.5 % (1.21) and XP-10 % (0.58), indicating that CDF can prevent Xuan paper from discoloration. Notably, Xuan paper is inherently resistant to aging and discoloration, and even after 20 d of UV aging, XP without protection still does not exhibit visual changes (ΔE* < 3). These are further supported by the fact that the optical images of Xuan paper (Fig. 5c) show almost no chromatic aberration.

Additional characterization methods were also used to further investigate the evolution of the composition and functional groups of Xuan paper during UV aging. The FTIR spectrum of XP shows similar peaks to that of BP (Fig. 5d). The OIIR of original XP is determined to be 0.0141. After UV aging for 20 d, the OIIR of XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 % is measured to be 0.086, 0.078 and 0.074, respectively. That means the CDF can also decelerate the oxidation of Xuan paper during UV aging. Fig. 5e and Fig. S3 show the UV–vis spectra and their first derivative graphs, respectively. In comparison with bamboo paper, the lower absorption of Xuan paper in the UV region may explain its slower UV degradation. Notably, XP-10 % shows more pronounced peaks at wavelengths of about 240 and 280 nm and a band at about 360 nm compared to XP and XP-0.5 %. The peak at about 240 nm is assigned to the hexenuronic groups formed during alkaline pulping, while the peak at 280 nm corresponds to the lignin in the pulp. Additionally, the band around 360 nm may consist of two individual peaks at 330 nm and 370 nm, assigned to coniferylaldehyde and para-quinones, which are considered as major contributors to the color of Xuan paper [46]. The photo-reduction of para-quinones in the presence of primary or secondary alcohol can lead to the formation of carbonyl groups [47]. The disappearance of the band at 360 nm in XP means the degradation of coniferylaldehyde and para-quinones, potentially explaining the bleaching of XP. However, the persistence of the peak at about 360 nm in XP-10 % suggests that CDF effectively retards the degradation of coniferaldehyde and para-quinone in Xuan paper under UV exposure, thus providing protection to the paper by absorbing UV radiation and preventing the degradation of paper constituent molecules. Moreover, XPS spectra of Xuan paper reveal two main elements, C and O (Fig. 5f). Fig. 5g-h show the C 1s XPS spectra of XP, XP-0.5 %, and XP-10 % after UV aging for 20 d, with the peaks divided into four peaks, consistent with those observed in bamboo paper. The results show that the sum of groups containing carbonyl (the peaks at 288.2 and 298.5 eV) measures at 13.06 %, 12.94 % and 12.74 % in XP, XP-0.5 % and XP-10 %, respectively, after UV aging for 20 d (Table S4). This is because that CDF can also prevent the formation of carbonyl groups in Xuan paper during UV aging. These findings indicate that CDF effectively protects Xuan paper from UV light damage.

In conclusion, our study presents an innovation application of carbon dot films (CDFs) for preventive conservation of paper-based relics against UV damage. These films are prepared through hydrothermal synthesis of CDs followed by mixing and drying with PVA, resulting in high tunable UV absorbance while maintaining minimal absorbance in the visible light range. With excellent UV absorption and stability, these films effectively shield the majority of UV rays while preserving the visual integrity of paper and paper-based artworks. Meanwhile, the UV transmittance of the film can be precisely adjusted by varying the amount of CDs, and the protective film can be easily removed from the paper surface without leaving any residue, offering customizable protection effects tailored to specific preservation and exhibition requirements. Supported by systematic characterization results, including pH value, degree of polymerization, color difference assessment, FT-IR, UV–vis, and XPS spectra, CDF effectively retards acidification, oxidation, and photocleavage induced by UV light in bamboo paper and Xuan paper. This work provides a practical and high-security method employing emerging carbon dots materials for the preventive conservation of paper against UV damage, addressing the diverse needs of paper and paper-based relics and artworks protection.

Mingliang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jinchan Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Sinong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Visualization, Conceptualization. Zhenyu Dai: Visualization, Formal analysis. Shuaitao Qin: Visualization. Shiliang Mei: Visualization. Wanlu Zhang: Supervision, Project administration. Ruiqian Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF0904200), Shanghai Rising-Star Program (23QA1404100), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 62074044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.08.134.
